name: Aínsa and Monastery of San Victorián Loop
name_original: Sierra Ferrera – circular por la cara sur
description: Cycle the moderate 24.8-mile (39.9 km) Sierra Ferrera circular route via the south face, gaining 2820 feet (860 metres) in 2h 23m.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Aínsa-Sobrarbe traverse the Spanish Pyrenees, offering diverse landscapes and challenging terrain. The region is characterized by dramatic topography, including limestone massifs, glacial valleys, and deep canyons, with routes often winding through well-paved surfaces. Cyclists encounter significant elevation gains and mountain passes, providing panoramic views of the Pyrenean range. The area is also defined by its rivers, forests, and ancient villages. Best road cycling routes around Aínsa-Sobrarbe The most popular road cycling route is Ordesa y Monteperdido – circular via Bajo Peñas, Cañón de Añisclo, Fanlo y Sarvisé (Revientachulos 2nd Half), a 64.5 miles (103.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 33 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Aínsa and Monastery of San Victorián Loop, a moderate 24.8 miles (39.9 km) path. This route offers a scenic ride around Aínsa, passing historical sites. Local road cyclists also love the Canyon of Añisclo from Aísnsa, a 41.2 miles (66.3 km) trail leading through the dramatic Añisclo Canyon, often completed in about 4 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Aínsa-Sobrarbe is defined by challenging mountain passes, deep canyons, and winding river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to difficult climbs. The routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 1,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Aínsa-Sobrarbe's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: Aínsa-Sobrarbe offers over 40 road cycling routes, catering to various skill levels. These routes traverse the Spanish Pyrenees, providing diverse landscapes and challenging terrain for cyclists.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: You can expect a dramatic and varied topography, including well-paved surfaces that wind through limestone massifs, glacial valleys, and deep canyons. Routes often feature significant elevation gains and mountain passes, offering panoramic views of the Pyrenean range, alongside rivers, forests, and ancient villages.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: Yes, while the region is known for its challenging climbs, there are a few easier options. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e923760012/ainsa-and-monastery-of-san-victorian-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aínsa and Monastery of San Victorián Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 24.8-mile (39.9 km) path that offers a scenic ride around Aínsa, passing historical sites.

Q: What are some of the most challenging road cycling routes in the region?
A: Aínsa-Sobrarbe is renowned for its demanding climbs. Challenging routes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926814485/ordesa-y-monteperdido-circular-via-bajo-penas-canon-de-anisclo-fanlo-y-sarvise-revientachulos-2nd-half" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ordesa y Monteperdido – circular via Bajo Peñas, Cañón de Añisclo, Fanlo y Sarvisé (Revientachulos 2nd Half)&lt;/a&gt;, a 64.5-mile (103.8 km) trail with significant elevation gain through the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e924180991/canyon-of-anisclo-from-aisnsa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canyon of Añisclo from Aísnsa&lt;/a&gt;, a 41.2-mile (66.3 km) trail through the dramatic Añisclo Canyon.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can encounter stunning features like the dramatic Añisclo Canyon, the Cinca and Ara rivers, and the nearby Lake Mediano. The area is also part of the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage site known for its impressive glacial valleys and diverse ecosystems.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to explore while road cycling?
A: Absolutely. The routes often pass through or near charming villages. Aínsa's Old Town, declared a Historic-Artistic Site, features a medieval quarter with cobbled streets, an 11th-century castle, and the 12th-century Santa María church. Other picturesque villages like Bielsa, Boltaña, and Abizanda also offer insights into Aragonese heritage.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: Aínsa-Sobrarbe benefits from a particularly mild climate with abundant sunshine and minimal rainfall, making it suitable for road cycling year-round. However, spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for longer rides and challenging climbs.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 30 reviews. Reviewers often praise the impressive landscapes, challenging mountain passes, and the well-maintained routes that wind through picturesque scenery.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926814485/ordesa-y-monteperdido-circular-via-bajo-penas-canon-de-anisclo-fanlo-y-sarvise-revientachulos-2nd-half" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ordesa y Monteperdido – circular via Bajo Peñas, Cañón de Añisclo, Fanlo y Sarvisé (Revientachulos 2nd Half)&lt;/a&gt; is a significant circular route, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e923760012/ainsa-and-monastery-of-san-victorian-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aínsa and Monastery of San Victorián Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate circular option.

Q: Can I find viewpoints or scenic spots along the routes?
A: The region is full of stunning viewpoints due to its mountainous terrain. Routes often provide panoramic views of the Pyrenean range, especially from mountain passes. You can also visit specific highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5954840" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bearded Vulture Observatory&lt;/a&gt; for incredible vistas.

Q: Are there any specific natural monuments near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area features several natural monuments. You might encounter geological formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/973469" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Picamartillo Cave&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1208961" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quizans Shelter&lt;/a&gt;. The confluence of the Cinca and Ara rivers also offers unique natural scenery.
tips:
The port of Arro leads to the Monastery of San Victorián, from the 6th century, considered the oldest in Spain.

Legend has it that Saint Victorián arrived in the Pyrenees fleeing from earthly temptations. After a life as a hermit in the Cueva de la Espelunga and realizing great prodigies, he was appointed abbot of the monastery, which was originally called San Martín de Asán. Some time later, in the 11th century, the monastery took its name and was renamed San Victorian or San Beturián.
Typical mountain village of Huesca that enjoys a panoramic view of the fantastic Peña Montañesa.
Located on top of a hill, this town has preserved its original structure and urban architecture from the 16th century practically intact, and is also an excellent viewpoint of the Peña Montañesa.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Sierra Ferrera circular route for road cycling?
faqs.answer: This route is considered moderate in difficulty. It features significant elevation gain typical of mountainous road cycling, with sustained climbs. While the average gradient might be around 5-7%, expect some steeper sections. It requires a good fitness level, similar to other challenging routes in mountainous regions.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and road surfaces can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows well-paved roads suitable for racebikes. However, as is common in mountain areas, some less-trafficked segments might have rougher patches. The terrain transitions from lower elevations to more exposed, rugged landscapes, offering varied scenery throughout.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable spots. These include the Puerto de Arro – Monastery of San Victorián, offering historical and scenic views. You'll also pass through Torrelisa and View of Peña Montañesa, and get another stunning perspective from the View of Peña Montañesa from El Pueyo de Araguás.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the Sobrarbe. BTT. Ruta 10. De Laspuña a Araguás, ZZ007 - Ruta Bajo Peñas, and Senderos del Sobrarbe, among others. This offers opportunities to explore further if you wish to extend your ride or combine it with other activities.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically from late spring through early autumn. During these months, the weather is generally more stable, and mountain passes are clear of snow. The south-facing roads benefit from ample morning sun, making for pleasant conditions. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or permits needed for cycling in Geoparque Sobrarbe-Pirineos?
faqs.answer: As this route is located within the Geoparque Sobrarbe-Pirineos, it's important to respect local regulations. Generally, no specific cycling permits are required for established routes like this one. However, always stay on marked paths, respect private property, and follow any signage regarding protected areas. There are no entrance fees for the Geopark itself.

faqs.question: Is the Sierra Ferrera route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and significant elevation gain, this route is generally not recommended for beginners or young children. It's best suited for experienced road cyclists with a good level of fitness who are comfortable with sustained climbs and descents in mountainous terrain.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details for this exact route name are not widely published, typical access points for circular routes in the region often involve parking in nearby villages or designated areas. Look for parking options in towns like El Pueyo de Araguás or near the Monastery of San Victorián, which are along the route.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so dogs are generally not a concern for the activity itself. However, if you plan to stop at any natural areas or villages, always keep your dog on a leash and be mindful of local regulations regarding pets in public spaces or protected natural areas within the Geoparque Sobrarbe-Pirineos.

faqs.question: What amenities like cafes or water stops are available along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through or near several small villages, such as Torrelisa and El Pueyo de Araguás, where you might find small cafes or shops to refuel. It's always wise to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer mountain rides, as services can be sparse between settlements. Look for historic springs or fountains in villages for water refills.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: For this moderate racebike route, ensure you have a well-maintained road bike, a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, and plenty of water and nutrition. Given the elevation changes, a good range of gears is essential. A basic repair kit (spare tube, pump, tire levers) and navigation tools are also highly recommended.
